figures.caption: Table 1: Instructions given to participants for each of the 15 tasks. We classified the 15 tasks into five categories: general knowledge, affordances, sensory experiences, affect, and future-casting.
Figure 4: t-SNE projections of MPNet embeddings, colored by task and task group. One model from each family (top performing, according to human-calibrated cosine distance) is shown for clarity.
Figure 3: t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) projections of embeddings (MPNet) colored by task (left) and image (right). Embeddings cluster by task as opposed to image.

Table 5: Chi square test differences between affordance constructions in image caption datasets versus WikiHow. Positive percent differences indicate more of a feature in WikiHow than a caption set. *** indicates adjusted p«0.0001.
Table 2: Statistical results on linear mixed effect model predicting HCD from task and prompt conditions. The intercept represents the general knowledge task, the human prompt, and the general tasks.

Figure 2: Comparisons between humans and VL models on NLP metrics taken from their respective generated descriptions. (A) Average description length was longer for VL models than humans. (B) Average Lexical Entropy was smaller for VL models than humans. (C) Average Type-to-Token Ratio was larger for humans than VL models. (D) Average Cosine Similarity was larger for humans than VL models until equating for description length (see text). (E) Average sentiment was more positive for VL models than humans. Error bars represent 95% confidence intervals.
Figure 6: Representative descriptions for two example images across a spectrum of HCD values for the navigation and sitting tasks.

Figure 7: The left side illustrates two basic characterizations of human-VLM differences tested in Section 1 of Experiment 2, while the right side illustrates the six mechanistic hypotheses tested in Section 2 of Experiment 2. For each, boxes are colored by the amount of evidence for each hypothesis and ranked from top to bottom by conceptual depth.
Figure 1: Representative human descriptions for each of the 15 tasks for two example images.

Figure 5: HCD distances among four different groups: Task Category, Task Generality, Prompt Type, and Model Family. Histograms show counts of HCD values, and curves represent kernel density for each group.
